refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, failover mechanisms, or specific communication protocols. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period_duration). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.999). Verify that measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before loss of availability occurs.
DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback control modes that preserve critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability events.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth and latency management with dynamic protocol selection based on measured link conditions to sustain availability above threshold.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) + quality monitoring (DP2) + fallback autonomy (DP3) in an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (3) hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous fallback modes, coordinated through unified state management and switchover logic.

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (fallback autonomy) share state synchronization requirements—tight coupling risk if not cleanly separated by explicit handoff protocols.
DP2 (monitoring) must feed both DP1 (failover trigger) and DP4 (adaptive management)—potential information coupling if monitoring logic is not abstracted as independent service.
DP3 (fallback autonomy) introduces functional coupling to remote operator intent—requires clear definition of autonomous decision boundaries to avoid ambiguity in control authority.
Acceptance criteria (≥0.999 availability) is time-dependent and measurement-dependent—coupling to evaluation period definition and telemetry infrastructure.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite and manages multiple failure modes. Coupling is acceptable if each sub-DP (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) maintains clear interfaces and state ownership. Risk: over-integration can obscure failure modes. Recommend explicit separation of concerns via IM layer (see below).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular, radio, satellite, wired). RISK: If only one pathway exists, DP1 (redundancy) cannot be realized; fallback to DP3 (autonomy) alone.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured and predicted with sufficient lead time (seconds to minutes) before availability drops below threshold. RISK: If degradation is abrupt, predictive detection (DP2) may fail; requires empirical validation.

ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous fallback modes (DP3) are acceptable to customer and comply with worksite safety plan. RISK: Regulatory or operational constraints may prohibit autonomous operation; requires explicit safety case.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative failure scenarios and achieve statistical confidence in ≥0.999 availability claim. RISK: Short evaluation windows may not expose rare failure modes.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation link is the only critical availability dependency. RISK: If power, sensor, or actuator availability is lower, overall system availability will be constrained by weakest link.

ASSUMPTION: Operator can tolerate latency and control mode transitions during failover events. RISK: If latency sensitivity is high (e.g., precision manipulation), failover may degrade task performance even if link remains available.

ASSUMPTION: State synchronization between primary and fallback pathways can be maintained with acceptable overhead. RISK: Synchronization traffic may consume bandwidth and introduce its own availability risk.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'link availability' (e.g., round-trip latency <X ms AND packet loss <Y% OR link down), (2) Acceptable control mode during link degradation (e.g., operator-supervised autonomy vs. full autonomy), (3) Constraints on failover latency and operator notification, (4) Dependency assumptions (e.g., power, sensors, actuators must also meet ≥0.999 availability), (5) Evaluation methodology (measurement points, failure injection, statistical confidence). DP5 should be decomposed into three independent sub-DPs at the IM layer: (IM-Redundancy) Redundant pathway manager with failover logic, (IM-Monitor) Link quality monitor with predictive alerts, (IM-Fallback) Autonomous control mode with operator override. Each IM should have clear ownership, interface contracts, and failure mode analysis.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability assurance system encompassing redundant communication pathways, real-time link quality monitoring, hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous fallback, and unified state management for switchover coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for redundant communication pathways, link quality telemetry, and failover signaling to/from physical network infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outbound interface reporting link quality metrics, predictive degradation indicators, and switchover state to monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound interface receiving operator commands and control directives; availability assurance system ensures reliable delivery through redundant pathways.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound interface signaling critical availability failures or fallback mode activation to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. Teleoperation is the primary consumer of reliable communication and control delivery. Redundant pathways, link monitoring, and switchover logic are core to maintaining operator-vehicle command fidelity. Local autonomous fallback aligns with teleoperation's need for graceful degradation when connectivity fails. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces cross boundary.

Poor containment. Safety Services is a consumer of availability assurance (via SafetyAlert), not the owner. The DP includes operational control logic (switchover, failover, hierarchical control) that is not safety-specific. Redundant communication and link monitoring are cross-cutting concerns that would diffuse across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and NGHE Solution if housed here.

Weak containment. Vehicle is the endpoint of control commands, not the orchestrator of communication assurance. Redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, and switchover logic are operator-side concerns. Placing this DP in Vehicle would require Vehicle to own communication infrastructure and failover decisions, creating tight coupling to teleoperation architecture and diffusing responsibility across vehicle firmware and teleoperation software.

Weak containment. NGHE Solution is a system-level container, not a functional module. Placing the DP here would blur ownership and make it unclear which subsystem (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services) is responsible for availability assurance. Diffusion would be high because the DP's logic would need to be distributed across multiple child modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of integrated availability assurance because it is the primary stakeholder in reliable command delivery and the logical locus of control over communication pathways, link monitoring, and switchover decisions. The DP's three pillars—redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, and hierarchical fallback—are all teleoperation-centric concerns that ensure operator commands reach the vehicle reliably. Unified state management and switchover logic belong in Teleoperation because they coordinate the operator's view of system health and control authority.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns other communication-related DPs (e.g., protocol handling, encryption). Mitigation: Ensure clear separation between availability assurance (this DP) and lower-level communication protocols (separate DP/IM).

Risk: Local autonomous fallback modes in Vehicle may need to be coordinated with Teleoperation's switchover logic, creating bidirectional coupling. Mitigation: Define clear handoff protocol via Network Link and SafetyAlert interfaces; Vehicle executes fallback autonomously, Teleoperation monitors and reacts.

Risk: Real-time link quality monitoring may require tight integration with network infrastructure (NGHE Solution level). Mitigation: Expose link quality data via Network Link interface; Teleoperation consumes and acts on it without owning the physical network.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may create unexpected coupling if Safety Services needs to veto switchover decisions. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as unidirectional notification; Safety Services reacts to availability failures but does not control switchover logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR by specifying a comprehensive solution architecture: redundant communication pathways ensure continuous connectivity; real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection enables proactive intervention before threshold breach; hierarchical control with local autonomous fallback maintains availability even during operator-vehicle link loss. The three pillars of DP5 are logically sufficient to achieve and sustain the customer-defined availability target (≥0.999). The DP is expressed at appropriate abstraction level for design domain reasoning and is testable against measured link availability metrics.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1 (DP<->IM Bridge): Unified state management and switchover logic are core to DP5 but risk fragmentation if Teleoperation module does not maintain a single source of truth. Mitigation: Enforce centralized state machine in Teleoperation for switchover coordination; all redundant pathways and fallback triggers feed into this single orchestrator.

Diffusion Risk 2 (DP<->IM Bridge): Real-time link quality monitoring spans both Teleoperation (consumer/decision-maker) and Network Link interface (data source). Risk of monitoring logic leaking into vehicle firmware or NGHE Solution infrastructure. Mitigation: Clearly separate data collection (owned by network layer, exposed via Network Link interface) from decision logic (owned by Teleoperation). Teleoperation consumes telemetry, does not own sensor implementation.

Diffusion Risk 3 (DP<->IM Bridge): Hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous fallback implies coordination between Teleoperation (operator-side switchover) and Vehicle (autonomous fallback execution). Risk of control logic duplication or unclear handoff. Mitigation: Define explicit protocol via Network Link interface; Teleoperation sends switchover directives, Vehicle executes autonomously and reports status. No bidirectional veto logic.

Diffusion Risk 4 (DP<->IM Bridge): SafetyAlert interface creates potential coupling if Safety Services attempts to influence switchover decisions. Risk of safety logic diffusing into availability assurance. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional SafetyAlert (Teleoperation → Safety Services). Safety Services reacts to notifications but does not control switchover; switchover decisions remain in Teleoperation domain.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). The DP is well-localized: redundant pathways, link monitoring, and switchover logic are all teleoperation-centric concerns that ensure operator command fidelity. External interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are clearly defined and unidirectional or bidirectional with explicit semantics. No evidence of DP scattering across Safety Services, Vehicle, or NGHE Solution. Containment hypothesis is validated: Teleoperation is the natural owner. Key success factor: maintain strict separation between availability assurance logic (Teleoperation) and lower-level communication protocols, network infrastructure, and vehicle autonomy (other modules).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion is measurable: link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period. Teleoperation module can expose Telemetry interface reporting real-time link quality metrics, switchover events, and fallback activations. Verification approach: (1) Instrument redundant pathways to log failover events and recovery times; (2) aggregate link availability data over evaluation period; (3) compare against customer-defined threshold. Predictive degradation detection can be validated through synthetic link degradation tests. Hierarchical fallback can be verified through controlled network isolation scenarios.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. Teleoperation module is unambiguously allocated ownership of DP5. Interfaces are explicit: Network Link (inout) provides redundant pathway access and link quality telemetry; Tasking (in) receives operator commands; SafetyAlert (out) notifies safety services of critical failures; Telemetry (out) reports availability metrics. Responsibilities are clear: Teleoperation orchestrates switchover, monitors link quality, triggers fallback, and maintains unified state. Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers, not owners. NGHE Solution provides network infrastructure but does not own availability assurance logic. No ambiguity in allocation or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define switchover latency requirement (e.g., 'failover detection and switchover execution shall complete within X milliseconds') as a sub-requirement of FR. This ensures DP5's redundant pathways and monitoring logic are designed to meet operator expectations for seamless handoff.

Revision 2: Clarify the protocol for local autonomous fallback coordination. Add a sub-interface specification: 'Vehicle Fallback Status' (inout via Network Link) to enable Teleoperation to query fallback state and Vehicle to report autonomous mode activation. This prevents diffusion of control logic.

Revision 3: Specify the content and frequency of Telemetry interface output (e.g., 'link quality metrics reported every 100ms; switchover events logged with timestamp and reason'). This ensures Teleoperation's monitoring data is consumable by external systems without requiring them to reverse-engineer state.

Revision 4: Add a constraint to DP5: 'Switchover logic shall not depend on Safety Services veto or approval.' This enforces unidirectionality of SafetyAlert and prevents safety logic from diffusing into availability assurance.

Revision 5: Document the separation of concerns between DP5 (availability assurance) and a sibling DP for 'Communication Protocol Handling' (e.g., encryption, serialization, error correction). Explicitly state that DP5 operates at the link-level abstraction and does not own protocol implementation. This prevents module bloat in Teleoperation.

Revision 6: Add acceptance criterion for worksite safety plan alignment: 'Availability assurance system shall not compromise operator situational awareness or safety decision-making during switchover events.' Ensure SafetyAlert interface is designed to enhance, not obscure, safety visibility.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is logically sound and well-contained. However, the recommended_revisions identify six critical gaps that must be addressed before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These are not design flaws but rather specification incompleteness that creates ambiguity in downstream implementation and verification. Given the high safety criticality of this system, proceeding to sub-level decomposition without resolving these gaps risks propagating ambiguity into lower-level FRs and creating verification blind spots. Revisions 1, 2, 3, and 4 are essential for FR and DP clarity; Revisions 5 and 6 are essential for IM boundary definition and safety alignment. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR-DP-IM triad will be sufficiently precise to support rigorous sub-FR derivation.

revise.why:
Six targeted revisions are required to eliminate specification gaps before sub-FR decomposition:

[FR-Level Revisions]
Revision 1 (FR): Add explicit switchover latency requirement (e.g., 'failover detection and switchover execution shall complete within X milliseconds'). Current FR states availability target (≥0.999) but does not specify the temporal constraint on switchover responsiveness. This is critical for operator trust and safety; sub-FRs cannot be derived without knowing the latency budget.

Revision 6 (FR): Add safety alignment constraint: 'Availability assurance system shall not compromise operator situational awareness or safety decision-making during switchover events.' This ensures SafetyAlert interface design and Teleoperation state transitions are safety-aware from the outset, preventing later rework.

[DP-Level Revisions]
Revision 2 (DP): Clarify the protocol for local autonomous fallback coordination by explicitly defining a 'Vehicle Fallback Status' sub-interface (inout via Network Link). Current DP5 states 'hierarchical control with local autonomous fallback' but does not specify the handoff protocol between Teleoperation and Vehicle. This ambiguity risks control logic duplication or unclear state synchronization in sub-level design.

Revision 4 (DP): Add explicit constraint: 'Switchover logic shall not depend on Safety Services veto or approval.' This enforces the unidirectionality of SafetyAlert and prevents safety logic from diffusing into Teleoperation's availability assurance domain during detailed design.

Revision 5 (DP): Document the separation of concerns between DP5 (availability assurance at link level) and a sibling DP for 'Communication Protocol Handling' (encryption, serialization, error correction). This prevents module bloat and clarifies the abstraction boundary for Teleoperation ownership.

[IM-Level Revisions]
Revision 3 (IM): Specify the content and frequency of Telemetry interface output (e.g., 'link quality metrics reported every 100ms; switchover events logged with timestamp and reason'). Current IM allocation is clear, but interface semantics are underspecified. Sub-level design of monitoring logic and data collection cannot proceed without knowing the telemetry contract.

All revisions are low-effort clarifications that strengthen the FR-DP-IM triad without requiring architectural rework. Once incorporated, the specification will be sufficiently precise to support decomposition of FR into sub-FRs for switchover latency, link quality monitoring, and hierarchical fallback coordination.

assumptions: Switchover latency requirement will be defined in collaboration with customer and safety engineering (Revision 1).
Vehicle firmware already supports autonomous fallback mode; Teleoperation's role is orchestration and state synchronization, not fallback implementation (Revision 2).
Safety Services module is a consumer of SafetyAlert notifications and does not participate in switchover decision logic (Revision 4).
Teleoperation module does not own communication protocol implementation; protocol handling is a separate DP owned by Network Link or NGHE Solution (Revision 5).
Telemetry interface is the primary mechanism for external visibility into Teleoperation state; no reverse-engineering of internal state is required by downstream consumers (Revision 3).
Operator situational awareness during switchover is a safety-critical concern that must be addressed in SafetyAlert interface design and Teleoperation state machine (Revision 6).
open_risks: Risk: Switchover latency requirement is not defined. Impact: Sub-FRs for link quality monitoring and failover detection cannot be optimized for responsiveness. Mitigation: Conduct customer workshop to establish latency SLA (e.g., 'switchover shall complete within 500ms') and allocate budget to monitoring interval, detection algorithm, and switchover execution.

Risk: Vehicle fallback protocol is ambiguous. Impact: Teleoperation and Vehicle may implement conflicting state machines, leading to control logic duplication or deadlock during switchover. Mitigation: Define explicit protocol specification (Revision 2) and validate through integration testing before sub-FR decomposition.

Risk: Safety Services coupling is not explicitly forbidden. Impact: Safety logic may diffuse into Teleoperation during detailed design, violating separation of concerns. Mitigation: Add constraint (Revision 4) and enforce via architecture review and code inspection.

Risk: Telemetry interface semantics are underspecified. Impact: Downstream consumers (e.g., monitoring dashboards, safety services) may misinterpret link quality data or miss critical switchover events. Mitigation: Define interface contract (Revision 3) and validate through integration testing.

Risk: Module boundary between Teleoperation and Network Link is not clearly defined. Impact: Availability assurance logic may leak into protocol handling or vice versa. Mitigation: Document separation of concerns (Revision 5) and enforce via interface contracts.

Risk: Operator situational awareness during switchover is not explicitly addressed. Impact: Switchover may occur without operator awareness, creating safety hazard or loss of trust. Mitigation: Add safety alignment constraint (Revision 6) and design SafetyAlert interface to enhance, not obscure, visibility.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined switchover latency requirement (e.g., from worksite safety plan or operator feedback).
Vehicle firmware specification for autonomous fallback mode (to validate Teleoperation's orchestration role).
Safety Services module interface specification (to validate unidirectionality of SafetyAlert).
Network Link interface specification for redundant pathways and link quality telemetry (to validate Teleoperation's data consumption model).
Telemetry interface contract (content, frequency, format) for external consumers.
Operator situational awareness requirements from safety engineering (to validate SafetyAlert design).
Integration test plan for switchover scenarios (to validate protocol correctness and latency compliance).
